title: Beckman Coulter DxU 850 Iris Urine Analyzer - Particle Recognition / Review Flag Issue
description: Troubleshooting excessive review flags, particle recognition concerns, image review issues, or questionable urine microscopy classification.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the DxU 850 Iris is not actively processing patient specimens before troubleshooting image review, particle recognition, or classification concerns. If urine microscopy testing must continue, have laboratory staff use another validated analyzer or follow the approved manual review process. Expected outcome: Questionable urine microscopy results are not released without proper review.

steps.title: Stop Reporting Questionable Results
steps.instructions: Ask laboratory staff whether patient results were affected by excessive review flags or unusual particle classification. Confirm whether results were held, manually reviewed, repeated, or verified according to laboratory policy. Expected outcome: Potentially misclassified results are controlled before release.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Recognition Issue
steps.instructions: Ask staff what they are seeing. Check whether the issue involves: Excessive review flags; Increased manual review workload; RBC, WBC, epithelial cell, cast, crystal, yeast, bacteria, mucus, or artifact misclassification; Unusual particle counts; Poor correlation with manual microscopy; Repeated abnormal flags on normal specimens; Abnormal images shown during review; Issue limited to one specimen type, one rack, or all samples. Expected outcome: The issue is defined as recognition, image quality, sample quality, operator review, or system-wide performance.

steps.title: Verify Sample and Specimen Quality
steps.instructions: Confirm the specimens are properly mixed, not excessively old, not visibly contaminated, and not outside laboratory acceptance criteria. Urine sediment can settle quickly, and poor mixing may cause inconsistent particle concentration. Expected outcome: Recognition concerns are not caused by specimen handling or settling.

steps.title: Check for Pattern by Sample Type
steps.instructions: Ask whether the review flags occur mainly with turbid urine, gross hematuria, heavy mucus, crystals, debris, or highly concentrated specimens. Some urine specimens naturally generate more review flags because particles overlap, clump, or obscure image fields. Expected outcome: Staff can distinguish true analyzer concern from specimen-driven review workload.

steps.title: Review Recent Reagent, Diluent, Cleaner, and Consumable Changes
steps.instructions: Confirm whether any fluids, consumables, controls, or sample containers were recently changed. Check for expired material, incorrect material, wrong lot in use, or improper storage. Expected outcome: Consumable-related causes are ruled out before assuming analyzer failure.

steps.title: Inspect the Sample Area for External Contamination
steps.instructions: With the analyzer idle and safe to access, visually inspect user-accessible sample areas for spilled urine, debris, dried residue, or rack contamination. Do not perform internal disassembly. Expected outcome: External contamination that could affect sampling or image quality is identified and corrected if accessible.

steps.title: Check Rack and Tube Presentation
steps.instructions: Verify that racks are loaded correctly, tubes are seated properly, and sample volume appears adequate. Poor tube position or insufficient sample volume can affect aspiration and downstream image quality. Expected outcome: Specimens are presented consistently to the analyzer.

steps.title: Check for Related Aspiration or Flow Errors
steps.instructions: Review recent analyzer messages or staff reports for aspiration errors, low flow, clogging, carryover concerns, or flow cell warnings. Particle recognition problems may be secondary to poor sample flow or debris in the sample path. Expected outcome: A possible fluidic or aspiration issue is identified if present.

steps.title: Review Image Quality During Manual Review
steps.instructions: Ask the operator to show examples of flagged images, if available. Look for: Blurry images; Dark or low-contrast images; Streaks; Bubbles; Debris; Excess background material; Inconsistent focus; Unusual image artifacts. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to particle recognition logic, poor image quality, or sample debris.

steps.title: Confirm QC and Background Checks Are Acceptable
steps.instructions: Ask laboratory staff whether current QC, background, or daily checks passed according to their procedure. Do not override QC failure or advise patient testing if quality checks are unresolved. Expected outcome: The analyzer is not used for patient reporting unless quality checks support use.

steps.title: Check for Recent Software, Database, or Review Rule Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether review rules, auto-verification settings, operator review criteria, middleware rules, or software settings were recently changed. Increased review flags may be caused by workflow or rule changes rather than analyzer hardware failure. Expected outcome: External configuration or LIS/middleware-related causes are considered.

steps.title: Compare to Manual Microscopy or Alternate Method
steps.instructions: Have laboratory staff compare affected specimens using the approved manual microscopy or alternate validated process. Clinical Engineering should not interpret patient results, but should document whether staff confirmed a correlation problem. Expected outcome: Staff determine whether the issue affects clinical result accuracy.

steps.title: Restart Only When Safe
steps.instructions: If the analyzer and workstation are idle, perform a controlled restart according to site procedure. Do not restart while specimens are processing or results are pending transmission. Expected outcome: Temporary software or processing issues clear without affecting patient results.

steps.title: Run an Approved Check or Control After Troubleshooting
steps.instructions: Have laboratory staff perform the appropriate QC, control, background, or verification process before returning the analyzer to normal patient use. Expected outcome: Analyzer performance is verified according to laboratory policy before release.

steps.title: Stop if Recognition Remains Questionable
steps.instructions: If particle recognition remains abnormal, review flags are excessive without explanation, images appear poor, or results do not correlate with manual review, stop troubleshooting. Expected outcome: The issue is escalated before inaccurate microscopy results are released.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory reported excessive review flags and questionable particle classification on the Beckman Coulter DxU 850 Iris urine analyzer.
CCR.Cause: Verified analyzer was producing repeated review flags; sample presentation, rack loading, consumables, and visible external contamination checks did not identify an external cause.
CCR.Resolution: Removed analyzer from service, labeled Out of Service, advised lab to use approved alternate testing process, and escalated unit for vendor/bench evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Whether QC or background checks passed
Specific review flags observed
Particle types being misclassified
Whether issue affected all samples or only certain specimen types
Example specimen conditions such as turbid, bloody, crystal-heavy, or mucus-heavy urine
Whether images appeared blurry, dark, streaked, or artifact-heavy
Rack and tube condition
Sample volume and mixing status
Any recent consumable, software, LIS, or review rule changes
Whether manual microscopy correlation was acceptable
Final device status

title: Beckman Coulter DxU 850 Iris Urine Analyzer - Workstation / Middleware / LIS Communication Failure
description: Troubleshooting DxU 850 Iris workstation, middleware, LIS, host connection, result upload, or worklist communication failures.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the DxU 850 Iris is not actively processing patient specimens before restarting the analyzer, workstation, network equipment, or middleware connection. If testing must continue, have laboratory staff use another validated analyzer or follow the approved downtime process. Expected outcome: Patient testing and result reporting are protected during communication troubleshooting.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Communication Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask laboratory staff what is failing. Check whether the issue involves: Results not reaching the LIS; Results stuck on the workstation; Worklist not downloading; Host connection error; Middleware rejection; Database or workstation delay; Analyzer communicating locally but not externally; Intermittent result transmission. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to analyzer-to-workstation, workstation-to-middleware, or middleware-to-LIS communication.

steps.title: Protect Result Integrity
steps.instructions: Confirm whether patient results are stored locally on the analyzer or workstation. Ask staff if any results were manually entered, repeated, or released during the communication outage. Expected outcome: Results are not lost, duplicated, mismatched, or released under the wrong patient record.

steps.title: Check for a Facility-Wide LIS or Network Issue
steps.instructions: Ask if other laboratory analyzers are also failing to transmit results or receive orders. If multiple instruments are affected, contact LIS, IT, or middleware support before assuming DxU 850 Iris failure. Expected outcome: A broader network or LIS outage is identified or ruled out.

steps.title: Verify Workstation Power and Status
steps.instructions: Confirm the DxU 850 Iris workstation or connected computer is powered on, responsive, and not frozen. Check monitor display, keyboard/mouse response, login status, and any visible error messages. Expected outcome: The workstation is available to receive, process, and transmit analyzer data.

steps.title: Check Analyzer and Workstation Connection Cables
steps.instructions: Inspect the communication cable between the analyzer and workstation, if applicable. Confirm cables are fully seated, undamaged, and connected to the correct ports. Expected outcome: Physical communication connections are secure.

steps.title: Check Network Cabling and Link Lights
steps.instructions: Inspect the Ethernet connection from the workstation to the wall jack, network switch, or designated network port. Look for link/activity lights at the network port when visible. Expected outcome: The workstation has a physical network connection.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Network Drop Is Being Used
steps.instructions: Verify the workstation is plugged into the approved laboratory network jack for the DxU 850 Iris system. Do not move the system to a different network port unless approved by IT or LIS. Expected outcome: The system remains connected to the validated network path.

steps.title: Restart Only When Safe and Approved
steps.instructions: If no specimens are running and staff approve, restart the workstation application first. If the application restart does not restore communication, restart the workstation according to local procedure. Avoid power-cycling the analyzer during active testing. Expected outcome: Temporary software or session communication faults are cleared.

steps.title: Confirm Host or Middleware Status on the Workstation
steps.instructions: Check whether the workstation shows host communication as online, offline, pending, disabled, or in error. Review visible connection indicators, message queues, or pending transmission lists if user-accessible. Expected outcome: The communication status is identified without changing validated configuration.

steps.title: Check for Results Waiting to Transmit
steps.instructions: Ask lab staff to confirm whether results are queued, held, rejected, or pending release. Do not delete, clear, or resend result batches unless following approved lab/LIS procedure. Expected outcome: Pending results are preserved and handled through the correct workflow.

steps.title: Confirm Patient and Specimen ID Workflow
steps.instructions: Verify that failed transmissions are not caused by missing specimen IDs, barcode errors, operator ID problems, order mismatch, or unverified results. Expected outcome: The issue is confirmed as communication-related rather than a specimen identification or workflow problem.

steps.title: Verify Middleware or LIS Acceptance
steps.instructions: Ask LIS or middleware support whether messages from the DxU 850 Iris are being received, rejected, delayed, or blocked. Common external causes may include interface downtime, rejected message format, inactive connection, changed host settings, or middleware queue issues. Expected outcome: The failure point is identified beyond the analyzer.

steps.title: Check Recent Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether there were recent changes to: LIS or middleware configuration; Network switch or wall jack; Workstation replacement; Windows updates; IP address assignment; Firewall rules; Instrument interface settings; Analyzer software updates. Expected outcome: A recent change is identified as a possible cause.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Test Transmission if Appropriate
steps.instructions: With laboratory and LIS approval, send a known test result, QC result, or approved interface test message if available. Confirm whether it appears at the workstation, middleware, and LIS. Expected outcome: The communication path is verified step by step.

steps.title: Do Not Make Unapproved Configuration Changes
steps.instructions: Avoid changing IP addresses, host settings, ports, interface protocols, or LIS destination settings unless directed by Beckman Coulter, LIS, IT, or approved site documentation. Expected outcome: The validated interface configuration is protected.

steps.title: If Communication Is Restored
steps.instructions: Confirm with laboratory staff that pending results transmitted successfully and no duplicate results were created. Document the corrective action and return the device to normal use if all testing and reporting functions are verified. Expected outcome: The DxU 850 Iris is safely returned to service.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory staff reported that DxU 850 Iris urine results were not transmitting from the workstation to the LIS.
CCR.Cause: Workstation and analyzer were operational, but the network cable at the workstation had no active link light and was found loose at the wall jack.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated the network cable, confirmed link activity returned, verified pending urine results transmitted with lab and LIS review, and returned the system to service.

helpfulDetails: Analyzer and workstation power status
Whether patient specimens were actively processing
Whether results were stored locally or queued
Exact communication error or host status shown
Workstation response or application status
Network cable condition and link lights
Wall jack or switch port used
Whether other lab instruments were affected
Middleware or LIS queue/rejection status
Any recent IT, LIS, software, or workstation changes
Test transmission result
Whether pending results were reconciled
Final device status
